data.title: AI And The Future Of Healthcare
data.description:
One of the most common questions among healthcare investors is how AI will impact current business models, from drug discovery and diagnostics to manufacturing. But there is another broader concern: AI has already disrupted many industries; will healthcare face the same threat?

The answer, as is often the case, is nuanced. Healthcare is not a single industry but a collection of disparate sub-industries, each with its own set of growth drivers and opportunities. AI has the potential to greatly improve workflows and efficiency across the healthcare ecosystem; however, I believe AI, rather than replacing entire subindustries, is more likely to serve as a powerful tool that can spur innovation, productivity, and long-term growth.

Why Healthcare May Adopt AI Differently

What makes the healthcare industry unique is the paramount need for safety and the overarching complexity of the regulatory environment.

Historically, technology companies have avoided heavily regulated industries such as healthcare. Instead, they produced highly effective tools—such as e-commerce, compute power, and in-silico simulations, which are computer-based experiments that virtually model medical processes—then partnered with healthcare companies well versed in the regulatory requirements necessary to succeed.

Therefore, I believe the most near-term logical path for AI in healthcare is the ability of healthcare companies to use AI through partnerships and acquisitions.

In addition, it is the more mundane aspects of healthcare (administrative tasks, revenue cycle management, billing, coding, and patient recruitment) where AI has made the most significant impact so far, despite more attention on using AI to discover new drug targets. Right now, AI is a more effective cost-savings tool rather than a revenue-generation tool, though I expect this to evolve over time.

Four Key Applications of AI in Healthcare

Today, the most successful and advanced uses of AI in healthcare can be put into four buckets: predicting disease, detecting and identifying disease, treating disease, and improving system efficiency. And it is in these four buckets where we believe AI will have the most significant near-term impact.

Predicting Disease

The predicting disease bucket focuses on consumer technology. That includes current wearables (such as the Apple Watch and Fitbit) and emerging devices such as Meta and EssilorLuxottica’s smart glasses, which can detect potential biomarker changes before disease onset.

While this segment is less regulated and primarily consumer-oriented, and some technologies are still in development, it represents the direction of AI in early disease prediction. These technologies can also directly benefit patients by increasing awareness of healthy habits and potentially changing patient behavior.

Detecting and Identifying Disease

AI has been widely used to help enhance diagnosis, particularly in radiology and cardiology, by leveraging pattern recognition in millions of medical images to detect abnormalities such as lung nodules, strokes, and fractures. There are two main approaches: hardware integration (“AI inside the scanner”) and software integration (“AI inside the workflow”).

The hardware integration approach is used by imaging equipment manufacturers, for instance, which are embedding AI in devices to produce faster, clearer scans and simplified operations. This allows scanners to automatically optimize image quality, reduce noise, and, in some cases, significantly shorten scan times. AI can also automate routine tasks such as positioning, image reconstruction, and preliminary measurements, making machines easier to use and reducing reliance on highly specialized technicians.

Meanwhile, the software integration approach is used by imaging software providers to help facilitate the transition from on-premises to cloud-based imaging, enabling the instant streaming of large imaging files and seamless AI solution integration within radiologists’ workflows. Hospitals also prefer embedded AI in existing platforms to avoid managing multiple vendors.

Treating Disease

AI is also being applied in surgical procedures, cancer treatments, and real-time clinical decisions, which are characterized by some of the highest regulatory scrutiny and barriers to entry in the healthcare industry. In these environments, AI is not replacing physicians but rather enhancing precision, improving planning, and enabling more consistent outcomes.

Several industry leaders are already embedding AI into their procedural ecosystems. For example, Intuitive Surgical's da Vinci robotic system uses AI for post-operative analysis and is progressing toward real-time surgical guidance with 3D overlays and semi-automated tasks such as suturing.

In addition, RaySearch Laboratories uses AI to help automate complex radiation treatment planning; and some dental companies have been incorporating AI into digital workflows for orthodontics and dental implants.

Improving System Efficiency

AI offers some of the most immediate opportunities in healthcare by helping streamline workflows, reduce waste, and accelerate research. Clinicians currently spend a significant portion of their time—often 25% to 40%—on documentation[1], while U.S. healthcare administrative waste totals an estimated $250 billion to $300 billion annually[2]. Clinical trials also remain lengthy and costly despite $300 billion in annual pharma research and development (R&D) spending[3].

And across these areas, AI is helping improve efficiency in tangible ways. In clinical trials, AI supports contract research organizations (CROs) and software firms in faster patient recruitment, site performance prediction, and automated data cleaning. In hospitals and health systems, AI can improve revenue cycle management by automating billing, coding, and claims processing. Meanwhile, in laboratories, life sciences tools companies are embedding AI into instruments to deliver faster, cleaner, and more reproducible results.

We believe improving efficiency and saving on administrative costs is the gateway for AI adoption in the healthcare industry. However, it will still require hospitals, health systems, and CROs to adopt it, given the high regulatory burden of the healthcare industry. Therefore, we believe AI remains a productivity-enhancing, not industry-replacing, tool.

Trial Duration Reduction With AI-Driven Site Selections

AI Can Help Reduce Phase 3 Recruitment Duration

Phase 3 recruitment time with AI

Opportunity, Not Displacement

We believe the excitement around AI in drug discovery is warranted, but there remains a significant barrier: AI is only as good as the underlying models that support it.

And to that end, humans today only understand about 10% to 15% of human biology. The human body is incredibly complex, with molecular-level interactions appearing seemingly random given the genetic and environmental factors that are unique to each human’s experience and DNA. Thus, until humans better understand biology, AI’s role in drug discovery and development will likely be limited.

That said, biopharma companies have been investing in AI capabilities for years, and early signs of meaningful productivity gains are beginning to emerge. One example is the development of “lab-in-the-loop” systems, where in-silico models inform wet lab experiments, and those results continuously refine the models. This feedback loop has the potential to accelerate discovery, enabling researchers to generate more targets in less time.

While still evolving, these advances could ultimately shorten development timelines and reduce costs, allowing biopharma companies to expand margins and reinvest in R&D. Large pharmaceutical companies could be particularly well positioned, given their scale, proprietary datasets, and deep scientific expertise.

At the same time, bringing a drug to market without completing human trials will be challenging for both regulators and companies because of safety risks. As a result, I believe there are still significant barriers to entry for AI to wholly replace pharmaceutical companies.

Until then, I believe AI will continue to have a profound impact on the efficiency of the healthcare system, on faster diagnoses, and on better prediction and treatment of disease.

Thus, on the global equity team, we have positioned our portfolio to leverage those healthcare companies that have the potential to rapidly adopt AI into their workflow and R&D process and create tangible benefits for their patients and their bottom line.

Footnotes

[1] Source: The National Institutes of Health.

[2] Source: McKinsey.

[3] Source: DeciBio.

Original Post

data.title: SandRidge Energy: Oil Production Expected To Increase 20% In 2026
data.description:
SandRidge Energy (SD) is projected to generate $64 million in 2026 free cash flow at current strip prices, helped by the 2026 oil strip exceeding $80. This is partially offset by weaker natural gas prices compared to when I looked at SandRidge in January.

SandRidge is now expected to end 2026 with over $150 million in cash before factoring in spending on share repurchases or acquisitions. I believe that SandRidge may attempt to acquire additional development assets since its current Cherokee inventory is on track to be used up in 2027.

I now estimate SandRidge's value at $13.20 to $15.75 per share in a long-term (after 2026) $75 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas environment. SandRidge's estimated value has increased by around $1.50 to $1.75 per share since I last looked at it, due to a $5 increase in my long-term oil price plus strong 2026 oil prices.

2025 Results

SandRidge reported solid 2025 results, with total production 4% above the midpoint of its guidance and oil production 1% above its guidance midpoint. It expected to exit 2025 at over 19,000 BOEPD in total production and reported approximately 19,500 BOEPD in Q4 2025 production.

This was achieved in conjunction with strong cost performance. SandRidge reported $40.5 million in full-year lease operating expense (after adding back the non-recurring, non-cash adjustments of operating accruals related to past periods). This was lower than its guidance for $42 million to $50 million in 2025 lease operating expense. As well, SandRidge's $69 million in 2025 capex was near the low end of its expectations for $66 million to $85 million in total capex.

SandRidge finished 2025 with $112 million in cash (including restricted cash). This was better than I expected due to the lower capex and strong operating cost performance.

SandRidge's 2026 Guidance

One thing to note is that SandRidge's 2026 production guidance has a wide range, particularly for oil production. SandRidge's total production guidance is for 6.4 million BOE to 7.7 million BOE, with the high end of that range 20% above the low end. SandRidge's oil production guidance is for 1.2 million barrels to 1.7 million barrels, which is a high-end that is 42% above the low-end.

SandRidge attributes a significant amount of that potential variance to the timing of new wells coming online. It is planning on continuing its one-rig Cherokee development program during 2026 with the aim of drilling 10 wells and completing 8 wells during the year. However, it notes that various factors (such as weather and crew availability) could shift wells later into the year. SandRidge also notes that the pooling process may result in it owning a higher working interest in some of its 2026 wells.

The working interest and well timing variability are also reflected in SandRidge's relatively wide capex budget range ($76 million to $97 million) for 2026.

2026 Outlook at Strip

SandRidge is aiming for approximately 20% oil production growth in 2026 at guidance midpoint, while total production may increase by 4%. SandRidge expects a minor decrease in its natural gas production and a minor increase in its production of NGLs.

The current strip for 2026 is approximately $83 WTI oil and $3.60 NYMEX natural gas. At those commodity prices, SandRidge is projected to generate $218 million in revenues after hedges.

SandRidge's 2026 hedges have slightly positive value overall, with some negative-value oil hedges offset by positive-value natural gas hedges.

I've modeled SandRidge's capex and operating costs at the midpoint of its guidance. SandRidge's operating costs came in better than guidance for 2025, so if it can repeat that performance, that would save it several million compared to the numbers below.

SandRidge is now projected to generate $64 million in 2026 free cash flow at current strip prices.

SandRidge's $0.12 per share quarterly dividend adds up to around $18 million in annual dividends, leaving $46 million in free cash flow after dividends for share repurchases and other purposes.

Notes on Cash Position

SandRidge ended 2025 with $112 million in cash (including $1 million in restricted cash). It would thus end 2026 with around $158 million in cash in this scenario, before spending on share repurchases and acquisitions.

SandRidge is expected to use up its current Cherokee inventory during 2027. As a result, it may make use of part of its substantial cash position to acquire more development assets.

Notes on Valuation

I have increased my long-term (after 2026) WTI oil price expectation from $70 to $75. At $75 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas, I estimate that SandRidge's PDP reserves (at the end of 2026) would have a PV-10 of approximately $470 million.

This results in a total valuation of $487 million to $581 million for SandRidge, based on 0.7x to 0.9x PDP PV-10 plus the $158 million in projected year-end cash.

Thus I now estimate SandRidge's value at approximately $13.20 to $15.75 per share. This is approximately $1.50 to $1.75 higher than my prior estimate of SandRidge's value, with the increase due to a $5 improvement in long-term oil prices plus strong 2026 oil prices.

Conclusion

SandRidge has been increasing its oil production with 32% growth in 2025 and another 20% growth expected in 2026. With strong 2026 oil prices, it is now projected to generate $64 million in free cash flow. This would increase its cash position to $158 million (before share repurchases or acquisitions) at the end of 2026, although it may decide to acquire additional development assets with its Cherokee inventory expected to run out during 2027.

The strong near-term oil prices plus a $5 increase in my long-term oil price improve my estimate of SandRidge's value by around $1.50 to $1.75 per share. I now estimate its value at around $13.20 to $15.75 per share.

data.title: SOXX: Buy The Picks And Shovels Of The AI/HPC Wave
data.description:
The iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) seeks to track the investment results of the NYSE Semiconductor Index.

SOXX enables an investor to have a concentrated exposure to the full semiconductor supply chain across designers, foundries, memory, and wafer fab equipment. With $24B in AUM, and a 0.34% ER, I am initiating SOXX on Seeking Alpha as a buy. Even with SOXX up 117% in the trailing 12 months, its holdings have an earnings growth runway that is of greater value than the risk of multiple compression.

Seeing that SOXX trades around an ATH, it is easy to call it expensive and move on. However, after looking at the holdings and looking at who is paying the companies in the basket, the analysis for me stopped being about multiple compression, and more so about how much earnings growth is going to overwhelm whatever compression shows up.

Capex to Hyperscalers

The setup for the thesis is on the demand side. CreditSights projects that the top five HPC companies (AMZN, MSFT, GOOG, META, ORCL) will spend approx. $602B on capex in 2026, which is up 36% YoY from 2025. Additionally, 75% of that is expected to be on AI infrastructure. The reason I am initiating a buy instead of a hold is the funding mechanisms. Capital intensity of these companies has reached 45-57% of revenue, which is a ratio you typically observe within utilities, not software companies. To fund this, hyperscalers raised about $108B in 2025, with MS and JPM projecting the sector to need $1.5 trillion in new debt over the next few years to continue the AI buildout. Once bonds are issued and funds are given to these companies, that money gets spent. GS projects a cumulative hyperscaler capex of approx. 1.15T from 2025-2027, which is double what was spent from 2022-2024. This is the huge transfer of corporate capital, and 75% of this flows directly into the SOXX basket.

Earnings

The backdrop from within the industry confirms the demand for the semiconductors industry. WSTS forecasts global semiconductor sales of approx. $975B in 2026, which is a 25% YoY, with logic and memory both projected above 30% growth. SIA puts 2025 sales at $791.7B. SEMI projects approx. $145B in equipment sales in 2026, which is up 9%, with wafer fabrication equipment growing 11% on HBM and logic capacity additions within Taiwan and Korea. Additionally, DRAM contract prices rose more than 90% sequentially within Q1 of this year. NAND prices also rose by more than half, and MSFT and GOOG have signed 3 year long memory contracts. SK Hynix describes 2026 as a transitional phase in a HBM led supercycle, with a global DRAM gap expected to persist through the end of the year in 2027.

Inside of SOXX

The top 10 holdings of SOXX are below:

Rank Ticker Company Weight What They Sell
1 NVDA NVIDIA 8.40% AI accelerators
2 AVGO Broadcom 8.27% Custom ASICs, networking
3 MU Micron Technology 6.99% DRAM, NAND, HBM
4 AMD Advanced Micro Devices 6.47% GPUs, EPYC CPUs
5 AMAT Applied Materials 5.84% Wafer fab equipment
6 MRVL Marvell Technology 5.17% Custom silicon, optical DSPs
7 INTC Intel 4.13% x86 CPUs, foundry
8 KLAC KLA Corporation 4.12% Process control, metrology
9 MPWR Monolithic Power Systems 4.08% Power management ICs
10 TER Teradyne 3.95% Semiconductor test equipment

The reason this basket earns a buy is not that semis are having a great year, but it is that every single name in the top 10 has a direct line to the hyperscaler capex I described above, and the contracts are already signed. Walking through it position by position is the actual thesis. NVDA at 8.40% is the cleanest expression of the buildout because it captures approx. 90% of accelerator spend and the company guided Q1 FY27 revenue at $78 billion off a customer base where hyperscalers are 50% of data center revenue. When AMZN, MSFT, GOOGL, and META are forced to spend the $400 billion of debt they raised to fund 2026 capex, the first wire transfer goes to NVDA. AVGO at 8.27% is the second wire transfer. The custom ASIC work for Google's TPU, Meta's MTIA, and OpenAI is doubling YoY, and the networking silicon that ties the GPU racks together is investment exposure on every AI cluster regardless of which accelerator wins. MU at 6.99% is where the buy thesis gets aggressive. MU is the only major U.S. memory pure play, and it sits in front of the HBM cycle that NVDA's Blackwell and Rubin platforms require to function at all. With DRAM contract prices up more than 90% in Q1 2026 and NAND up more than 50%, the EPS math on MU alone could clear 100% YoY, which is enough to push earnings growth into double digits before any other position contributes a dollar. AMD at 6.47% is the secondary accelerator bet, with management guiding 60%+ data center growth annually over the next few years on MI350 ramps to MSFT, META, ORCL, and other AI customers. Even if AMD captures only 10% of the accelerator market against NVDA's 90%, the dollar growth is still enormous because the market itself is growing 40%+. AMAT at 5.84% and KLAC at 4.12% are picks and shovels of the picks and shovels. Think of it like a matryoshka doll. They sell the wafer fab equipment that the foundries are buying right now to build the capacity that will produce the chips that NVDA, AVGO, MU, and AMD will sell into the next leg of the cycle. SEMI's $145 billion 2026 equipment forecast is the line item that lands on AMAT's and KLAC's income statements.

The next four positions are why I prefer the SOXX modified weighting structure to the more concentrated alternatives. MRVL at 5.17% is the under the radar AI play that does not get the press NVDA does. Marvell does the custom silicon work for AWS and Microsoft and sells the optical DSPs that go into the high-speed interconnects every AI cluster requires. As the buildout pivots from compute to networking and storage, MRVL is the cleanest beneficiary in the basket, and it is a position that SMH does not give you at this weight. INTC at 4.13% is the one position in the top 10 that I have the least conviction on. The foundry turnaround is a real positive catalyst, but it is also genuine execution risk, and a single bad earnings print can mark this position heavily, and quickly. INTC is the reason this is a buy and not a strong buy. MPWR at 4.08% is small but matters more than the weight suggests because Monolithic Power sells the voltage regulators that step rack power down to what GPUs consume. Every NVDA system shipped contains MPWR content, which makes it a volume play that compounds with the buildout. TER at 3.95% is another big winner in the basket. Teradyne sells the test equipment that every chip on every wafer must pass through before shipment, which means it does not matter who wins the accelerator war. NVDA, AMD, AVGO, and Marvell silicon all flow through Teradyne handlers on the way out the door, and rising chip output is rising Teradyne revenue.

The blended earnings growth across the top 10 lands in the 32% to 38% range. That is not a super optimistic forecast, and it does not require a single name to deliver a beat. It just requires the contracted capex on the demand side to get spent, and the hyperscalers do not have a mechanism to not spend it because the debt is already on their balance sheets. The lower end of that EPS range, 32%, against the 15% multiple compression I am modeling, still produces a 17% total return in the base case, which is what gets SOXX into the buy band against the S&P 500.

Tradability

SOXX has a high volume of approx. 8 million shares a day, with a tight bid-ask of about 1 cent. The options chain is very liquid, even incorporating a healthy chain involving LEAPS.

SOXX is also marginable, which matters if this is a leveraged expression within a portfolio. SOXX is very tradable, very liquid, and a very attractive option not only to buy, but attractive as you are able to buy it easily.

Downside Risk

Deloitte's 2026 outlook flags the right risks: more efficient AI models reducing per task compute demand, new competing accelerators creating price pressure, and the historical reality that AI chip margins at current levels do not stay at current levels indefinitely. None of those have to happen right away, they just have to be priced in. I would also flag memory as a risk as well. If DRAM contract prices roll over early, instead of holding over to the rest of the year, EPS falls dramatically in the SOXX basket thanks to MU's lagging in this scenario, and the bull case turns into a hold or sell. A meaningful drop in hyperscaler capex would also warrant concern and cause a downgrade from myself as well.

Takeaway

SOXX is a buy, along with buys to thematic semiconductor exposure. The contracted hyperscaler capex in 2026, funded with longer dated debt that must be spent, flows directly into the collection of the SOXX ETF's investments of accelerators, ASICs, HBM, and wafer fabrication equipment. With the entire portfolio poised for strong earnings, I believe SOXX will outperform the broader market. While the basket is not cheap, it does not need to be. Whoever wins the AI race, and whoever loses, will both be buying from the SOXX basket, and that is enough for me to be a buyer, even at more expensive levels.

data.title: Austal: Strong Buy Intact, Free Cash Flow Remains The Key Risk
data.description:
Austal (AUTLF) has lost nearly 10% of its value since I upgraded the stock to strong buy. Since the war in Iran, the stock market has been under pressure, and names in the aerospace and defense industry have not been an exception. In this report, I discuss why the share prices of Austal have been under pressure despite increased tensions globally seemingly being a positive for names such as the Australian naval shipbuilder.

Near-Term Pressures Outweigh Benefits Of Increased Tension

Austal share prices had already been under pressure prior to my last report due to an accounting issue on the T-ATS program requiring the company to lower its FY2026 guidance. Since the start of the war, Austal stock has lost around 9% of its value compared while the S&P 500 lost around 1% and the Aerospace and Defense ETF ITA lost around 6% of its value. So, it is clear that aerospace and defense names have not been favored by the market.

There are several reasons for that. The first one is that aerospace and defense stocks have outperformed in recent years as global tensions rose globally. The easy way to look at the escalation in Iran is that this is another conflict that highlights the need for expanding defense budgets and weapon system procurement. The reality, however, is that more escalation zones are not adding to the near-term upside for many aerospace and defense companies. Aerospace and defense companies already cannot fulfill the current demand as capacity and supply chain are the limiting factors. So, the near-term contract upside is rather limited. What additional conflicts do is add to the longer-term stability of revenues or growth. It does not mean that near-term growth is going to be higher. Additionally, demand is mostly focused on missile defense, missiles, drones, and counterdrone solutions. So, not in the realm of Austal.

At the same time, the conflict brings risks or investor-perceived risks for Austal and many other companies. Energy prices have increased, and inflation seems to be increasing again. The result is that while there is no near-term upside, current programs are seeing increased risks on cost execution.

Defense contracts are typically structured as fixed-price contracts with escalation clauses or cost-plus contracts. So, some inflation can be passed through. However, structural increases in, for instance, material costs are more difficult to offset. Commercial ships are a smaller part of the business and have even less shielding. So, the risk for Austal is that while inflation may be passed through in some cases, higher input costs for energy-intensive processes such as the manufacturing of steel and aluminum are likely not part of the pass-through mechanism, meaning that down the chain cost of goods can increase, and that also affects Austal, while shipping costs may also increase overall costs and affect timelines.

Coming out of the pandemic and with the energy crisis sparked by the war in Ukraine, we saw inflation and input costs increase for many defense contractors, forcing them to recognize significant charges. That fear is now driving the downward stock price reaction for Austal as well.

At the same time, we do note that a somewhat lasting GDP impact would not result in raw material prices keeping rising. They could be elevated due to the cost floor moving up, but that same cost floor moving up would result in demand decreasing, forcing prices down and smelters to temporarily reduce output, creating an input cost at a potentially higher base. So, currently it does seem like the stock is down by the market mechanisms, but demand is unlikely to be lower for Austal ships, and the way the supply chain is oriented any cost would likely hit 1.5-3 years from now, and by that time the economic impact that the war may have may have resulted in a near-term spike in raw materials, such as aluminum prices being hiked 12%, but a high price point eventually may result in demand destruction leading to a stable to slightly higher material cost profile.

So, I am not expecting a collapse of Austal’s margins, but we are watching any comment on capital expenditures and input costs that the company may have.

Austal Remains A Strong Buy In My View

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Austal, my expectations have not substantially changed. There is some downward pressure in FY27, but overall that only drove the price target from $5.20 to $5.11 and still implies 49% upside. The main risk would be any accumulation of working capital and higher input costs, which could erode free cash flow. Currently, we have no immediate reason to assume that this will also be affecting the company in the near-term. Given how extensive the supply chains and inventories are, price increases today would affect the company 1.5-3 years from now.

While I do believe that the impact of higher input costs could be relatively limited for Austal, we do note that the company already does not have a nice streamlined profile when looking at the financial metrics. Margins were already expected to dip this year regardless of the situation in Iran and recover to prior levels in the years after. The company is going through higher capital expenditures, and that is also where they may be exposed to inflation and input cost challenges and is the second reason, next to input costs for Austal products, to keep an eye on free cash flow. The company remains in a net cash position, but that net cash position is driven by the capital raise last year.

Conclusion: There Are Risks But I Believe Austal Will Manage

Higher raw material costs could eventually affect Austal, but that will not be in the near term, given that higher raw material costs first have to flow through the supply chain and inventory. So, there is a delayed effect, and it is more than likely that many companies are now already looking at how to deal with higher input costs. So, by the time higher input costs should clearly start affecting the business, I also believe that Austal will have explored flexibility in its commercial contracts to offset higher costs. So, I am maintaining my strong buy rating, counting on the inventories providing a buffer against higher raw material costs now and mitigations that can be studied to keep future impact manageable.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Kelly Services: The Stock Nobody's Talking About
data.description:
Kelly Services: The Stock Nobody's Talking About

Most people my age remember the Kelly Girl the way they remember Xerox machines and three-martini lunches — a fixture of a business era that feels both recent and impossibly distant. The Kelly Girl showed up when your admin called in sick, when tax season buried the accounting department, or when the boss needed a dozen people to stuff envelopes by Friday. Not Lucy Ricardo fumbling around at the chocolate factory, more like the competent woman Lucy always wished she could be. Efficient. Unflappable. Suspiciously cheerful about the whole thing.

William Russell Kelly started this in 1946 in Detroit. Not trying to reshape the American labor market — just running a small office services shop, handling typing, copying, and basic calculations for clients. Then those clients started asking if the workers could come to them. Kelly said sure, and accidentally tapped into one of the biggest underused labor pools in postwar America: housewives, students, and women who wanted a paycheck but weren't getting recruited for the corner offices.

By 1964, Kelly (KELYA) had 169 offices across 44 states. The name "Kelly Girl" had become shorthand for any temp worker, regardless of gender or employer. The company hit $1 billion in sales by 1986. Fortune 500 by the mid-2000s. On Wall Street though, the story looked different. The stock peaked at $38.75 in September 1997, right at the height of the dot-com hiring frenzy. It has been grinding lower for nearly three decades since.

So why is anyone looking at this now?

Kelly is at $8.71 as of April 10. Down 51% since 2023. Sitting just above a 52-week low of $7.98. Its market cap is roughly $305 million against $4.3 billion in annual revenue. That works out to about 0.07x sales and 0.31x book value. You're paying roughly 31 cents for a dollar of accounting assets. That multiple has one standard interpretation: the market thinks the cash runs out before anything gets fixed.

What's harder to reconcile with that read is $114.1 million in free cash flow generated in 2025, operating cash of $122.6 million minus $8.5 million in capex. The prior year that number was $15.8 million. A sixfold jump in free cash flow, in a year where revenue was down, suggests the cost structure got leaned out hard and fast. At a $305 million market cap the stock is trading at under 3x free cash flow.

The market is either right that this thing falls apart in the next 12 months, or it's simply checked out.

The valuation gap is embarrassing

Competitor Kforce is trading at 11x EV/EBITDA. Kelly is at 3.8x. ManpowerGroup, which is dealing with its own revenue headaches, still gets 6x. The staffing sector doesn't command great multiples right now, nobody's arguing that, but there's a meaningful difference between "cyclically depressed" and "priced for extinction," and Kelly is currently getting the latter. Barrington Research has a $15 price target on the stock. The broader analyst consensus sits at $16.67. That's 91% above where it's trading today, and it's not built on anything heroic. Instead, it's built on Kelly getting priced like a staffing company that's still standing rather than one that's winding down.

Nobody pays 4x to be a spectator

In January 2026, Hunt Equity paid $106 million for 92% of the voting control via the Class B shares, nearly 4x the trading price of the publicly available non-voting stock at the time. Nobody wires $106 million for a 400% premium and then waits to see what happens.

Then in late February and March, CEO Chris Layden and director James Christopher Hunt went into the open market and bought shares at the same price anyone else was paying. Hunt picked up 15,000 shares across three separate transactions in February alone — 1,000 at $9.58, 10,000 at $9.57, 4,000 at $9.30. These are people with full access to the books, the pipeline, and a pretty good sense of what Q1 guidance was going to look like. They bought anyway.

GRACE Boost and the IT work that actually matters

Kelly's AI platform is called GRACE Boost AI. The pitch is straightforward: automate the administrative grunt work so specialized recruiters can focus on Science, Engineering and Technology placements, where the margins are. Less time on LinkedIn. More time on relationships that close.

In Q4 2025, Kelly finished consolidating all its SET acquisitions onto a single unified technology platform. For years the company had been running acquired businesses on mismatched legacy systems, a drag that doesn't show up cleanly in any line item but shows up everywhere in recruiter productivity and data quality. Getting onto one stack is the precondition for deploying AI across the global business rather than in isolated pockets. One concrete output: Kelly helped a technology manufacturer scale U.S. production from 10,000 to 4 million units annually by building out a workforce alongside the company's robotics deployment. Either that's a real shift in what Kelly is selling, or it's a good slide in a pitch deck. The Q2 and Q3 numbers will start to tell you which.

The part of the business nobody talks about

While the debate rages about whether AI kills staffing, Kelly has quietly built something in Education that doesn't get nearly enough attention. Substitute teachers, therapists, and K-12 support staff now account for 28% of total revenue — roughly $1.2 billion annually at current run rates. That segment grew 1.3% year-over-year in Q4 2025 while ETM was down 18.6% and SET was down 11.3%.

School districts don't freeze headcount the way corporate America does. They have a different budget cycle, different procurement process, and a different relationship with the companies that serve them. Kelly recently relaunched Pediatric Therapeutic Services as Kelly Pediatric Therapy — treating it as a standalone growth business rather than a legacy line item. At current revenue levels that's a $1.2 billion operation that, if it were standalone, would trade at a meaningfully higher multiple than 0.07x sales.

About that earnings miss

Adjusted EPS came in at $0.16 in Q4 against a $0.45 consensus. That's a 64% miss. Gross profit margin landed at 18.8%, down 150 basis points year-over-year, with management pointing to healthcare and workers' compensation cost escalations. Those aren't one-time items in the way a restructuring charge is. Staffing businesses with heavy blue-collar and healthcare worker exposure carry structural cost risk in exactly these categories, and there's no obvious lever that makes it go away quickly. Bears who say these are permanent headwinds rather than cyclical ones aren't wrong to ask the question.

Q1 2026 guidance calls for revenue down 11-13% year-over-year with adjusted EBITDA margin around 1.5%. Management separates the "underlying" decline of 3-5% from the reported number by stripping out the federal contractor exposure and three large lost commercial customers — a legitimate distinction, but also exactly the kind of footnote that tends to make skeptical analysts more skeptical rather than less.

The GAAP net loss of $128.8 million, or $3.69 per share, included a $127.9 million non-cash tax valuation allowance. That's an accounting judgment about whether the company can use its deferred tax assets;not cash out the door. Adjusted net earnings were $5.9 million. Still a miss, but not evidence of a company in freefall.

What the stock is worth and why

Near term the math is simple. If the bleeding stops at that 3-5% underlying decline rate and the stock re-rates off distressed multiples toward something resembling a normal staffing company, you're looking at $13 without needing anything to actually go right. That's 0.10x forward revenue, still a discount to every peer in the sector, still under 3x free cash flow. $13 isn't a recovery — it's the stock price of a staffing company that generates $114 million in cash annually and isn't actually dying.

The analyst consensus at $16.67 implies EBITDA margins recovering back toward 3% — where they were running in Q1 2025 before the SET and ETM deterioration — and the market assigning a 7x multiple on roughly $130 million in EBITDA, which is mean reversion, not optimism.

The two-year case at $22+ requires GRACE Boost to actually show up in gross margins and Education to keep doing what it's doing. If Education reaches 32-33% of revenue at 2-3% annual growth while SET stabilizes near flat, Kelly exits 2027 with a revenue base closer to $4.4 billion and EBITDA margins approaching 3.5%. Run that at 8x EV/EBITDA on $154 million of EBITDA and you get to the low-to-mid $20s — still a discount to Kforce at 11x, on assumptions that require almost no recovery in the cyclical segments.

The variable nobody's pricing in yet is Pat McCall, the new Chief Growth Officer who came over from AMN Healthcare where he stabilized a similar post-downturn business. If the "One Kelly" go-to-market push he's been brought in to execute actually lands with large enterprise clients, the revenue recovery comes sooner than the model assumes.

One last reinvention

The Kelly Girl has changed her look more than once in 80 years. What's different in 2026 is that the reinvention is happening while the stock trades at under 3x free cash flow, with a controlling shareholder who paid 4x market price to get a seat at the table and management buying in the open market alongside everyone else.

At $8.71, the market has priced in the worst version of the story. If even one piece goes right — Education holds, margins stabilize, McCall wins back a couple of the lost enterprise accounts — the current price looks like a mistake in retrospect.

If none of it goes right, there's still $114 million in free cash flow. At some point, someone does that math.

data.title: The Davenport Funds Insider Buying Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Q1 2026 Market Review

The Davenport Insider Buying Fund (DBUYX) gained 2.73% during the first quarter compared to a decline of 4.33% for the S&P 500 ® Index. The market's proverbial "wall of worry" now includes a few additional partitions, including war in the Middle East, a back-up in bond yields, and increasing concern about the ramifications of artificial intelligence.

Fund Update

Contributors: The Fund's top contributors during the fourth quarter were Darling Ingredients, Inc. (DAR), ConocoPhillips (COP), and Keysight Technologies, Inc. (KEYS). Entering the quarter, Conoco was the Fund's second-largest holding, albeit not based on any premonition about war in the Middle East. That the stock sat near the top of the quarterly leaderboard after oil prices surged 78% is unsurprising. While Conoco's largely domestic resource profile should be an increasingly valuable asset, nearly half its production is natural gas, for which domestic prices declined -8% in the quarter. It's also worth highlighting that 10 different insider sales occurred at Conoco in March, subsequent to the outbreak of the war (including $79.5M by its CEO), and we chipped about half our position into the strength as well. While oil prices may remain "higher for longer," we also want to respect both the insider signal and the cyclicality that commodities often exhibit. Elsewhere, Darling benefited from the US government finalizing Renewable Volume Obligations for 2026 and 2027, which should drive significant growth in renewable diesel – Darling is a 50% owner of Diamond Green Diesel, one of the nation's largest biofuel refiners. Keysight posted a sizable beat-and-raise quarter, with several of its heretofore moribund markets beginning to show signs of improvement.

Detractors: The Fund's top detractors during the quarter were Builders FirstSource, Inc. (BLDR), TransUnion (TRU), and Wynn Resorts Ltd (WYNN). Rising interest rates conspired against housing and credit applications, which hurt Builder and TransUnion, respectively. Private fixed residential investment sits near multi-decade lows, with an aging stock of existing homes and demand exceeding supply both pointing to incremental housing demand – eventually. As for Wynn, the war in the Middle East stretched into the United Arab Emirates, where Wynn has been completing its latest casino mega-resort. Higher oil prices also sap disposable income, which could result in less travel and gambling activity in Wynn's existing markets, such as Las Vegas and Macau.

Fund Activity

During the quarter, we initiated three new positions: Abbott Laboratories (ABT), Microsoft Corp (MSFT), and Broadridge Financial Solutions Inc (BR). Considering how well-known Microsoft is, we'll skip ahead to its near-30% decline from its peak amid a software industry meltdown (fears include artificial intelligence driving lower white collar employment, and therefore fewer users of software). MSFT shares reached their lowest valuation in about a decade. Meanwhile, Microsoft appears well positioned via its minority ownership stake in OpenAI, as well as its sizable market share in data centers/cloud computing. A long-tenured Board member purchased $2M and insider selling in 2026 dried up, both encouraging signals, in our view. Broadridge, which had been a steady and solid compounder over long periods of time, also appears to be a near-term victim of the software malaise. Its software enables mission-critical collateral management and trade processing for banks and brokerage firms, while its investor communication business delivers proxy statements and shareholder votes. “Democratization” of financial markets via fractional share ownership, ETFs, and online trading have driven “position growth” that’s compounded at 9% annually for the past decade – a key driver of growth for Broadridge that seems likely to persist. The company’s CEO recently made his first-ever insider buy: $1 million. Abbott is a diversified healthcare company, serving end markets across medical devices, diagnostics, established pharmaceuticals, and nutrition. Its recent acquisition of Exact Sciences expands its diagnostics addressable market, and should accelerate growth. Abbott’s stock had declined after the company reported a lackluster December quarter result, with the Nutrition segment’s -9% organic revenue growth being the key culprit. Meanwhile, total company organic growth was +3.8% in the quarter and +5.5% for the year, with 2026 guided to accelerate toward +6.5 to +7.5% on the back of new product launches. The CEO purchased $2M+, followed by a $1M+ purchase from a long-tenured Board member.

We fully exited Charter Communications, Inc. (CHTR) and Zimmer Biomet Holdings, Inc. (ZBH), where prospects for fundamental improvement seemed to be shifting to the right. We also sold our stub position in Waters Corp (WAT), shares of which we received from our ownership of Becton-Dickinson & Co (BDX). The biosciences business that BDX distributed to Waters had been BDX’s slowest-growing unit and the price BDX received for it seemed full/fair. In addition to the shares of WAT we received, WAT also paid BDX $4 billion, which BDX is using for debt paydown and share buybacks.

In recent months, a number of our holdings have received attention from activist investors. LKQ Corp (LKQ) announced plans to consider a range of potential outcomes including a sale of the entire company. In a similar vein, Cooper Companies (COO) announced it has initiated “a formal strategic review to enhance long-term shareholder value.” More recently, a new activist shareholder has made rumblings at Align Technology, Inc. (ALGN). These activists presumably also see value beyond what the market currently ascribes, and are likely to hold management’s feet to the fire in terms of delivering that value.

Conclusion

In past letters, we’ve called out the Fund’s ownership of small- and mid-sized companies, which posted better performance in the most-recent quarter. As always, insider buying guides the opportunity set for us – when there’s more insider buying in any particular corner of the market (value/growth, small/large, various sectors/industries), we will explore those opportunities and act accordingly.

Sincerely,

Adam Berman, CFA®, Co-manager, Insider Buying Fund

Kevin C. Bennett, CFA®, Co-manager, Insider Buying Fund

data.title: The Allstate Corporation: Good Value, But Growth Too Dry
data.description:
As one of the largest publicly traded property and casualty insurance companies (P&C) in the United States, The Allstate Corp. (ALL) has had a volatile year as a stock, while enduring some massive earnings gains in the meantime. The firm sits 4th among peers in auto market share and 2nd in homeowners market share.

I have ALL as a hold. Net premiums earned are gaining steadily, but not as much on the auto front, which is the largest revenue element for P & C firms. Their market share has been stagnant besides some slight improvement in homeowners. Their profitability profile has improved, as underwriting gains were a big success driver last year. The stock is trading at a discount, and we see growth through margin expansion. But I just don't see enough upward trend in their net premiums and largest segment in auto insurance.

Net Premium Business

Net premiums earned at ALL have continued to show steady improvement, reflecting both pricing strength and modest policy growth across its core segments. Premiums earned in auto are up about 2% YoY, with homeowners taking a much bigger leap up around 14%. Net premiums earned made up over 90% of their total revenue over the last 5 years and are the backbone of their business.

CEO of ALL, Tom Wilson, says that while premiums on a macro level were raised this past year, leading to further income, they also were able to reduce premiums for 7.8 million auto and home customers in 2025. This was by an average of 17% less on their policies, hailing from tailored coverage reviews that efficiently offset costs of inflation. Management commentary has alluded to net premium growth sustaining but remaining moderate over the next year.

Profitability Profile

ALL has undergone a notable transformation in its profitability stance. The company has focused on improving underwriting margins through more precise pricing. This has reflected a significant improvement to their combined ratio. This is the key metric in insurance between underwriting costs and earned premiums.

Rate increases have outpaced underlying claims inflation after several years of higher loss severity. It seems to me that they have shifted their business approach from pure growth to a more disciplined focus on risk-adjusted returns and margin improvement. They have done a fantastic job on this, as catastrophe losses dropped 49% QoQ, while they increased their market positioning in homeowners in riskier areas prone to more catastrophic weather.

Their homeowners combined ratio moved from 90.1 to 84.4 last year. The catastrophic management has been their biggest area of growth and improvement as a company in the last year.

Overall margins have ticked higher now for almost 10 quarters, after 2023 saw large declines on larger than normal catastrophic claims. On one hand, their 40% gross margin and 15% net income margin are both below the sector median. On the other, they are right in line with their specific peers and are emphasizing this as a core business tactic moving forward.

Valuation Strong

This is an enticing stock from a valuation standpoint. Their PE sits below 6x and has continued to look more appetizing after big beats in earnings each of the last few quarters. They are valued slightly below direct peers like Progressive (PGR) and Travelers (TRV) and support an exceptional PEG ratio of 0.04x. Lastly, they are trading at less than half the value they were a year ago, making this stock undeniably likable from a value perspective.

What I Don’t Like

The commitment to prioritizing profits over volume has been evident for ALL. That can be great, but the piece I don’t like about this is that a year or two of catastrophic weather or events could do big damage to the firm's bottom line. Their increase in underwriting income could fall sharply again or turn into a loss (like in 2023) if large sudden losses occur. This leads to more claims paid and a worse combined ratio. As odd as it sounds, I think their growth strategy seems too reliant on good weather.

Their net premiums earned are growing, but it has softened, and they are not seeing as fast of advances as many peers. This has led to market share in auto remaining flat. As the biggest segment of revenue for all P&C insurers, I would like to see them attack this side of the business with more vigor.

They also gained $59 million more in net investment income in 2025 than the year prior. Of course, this is great for bottom-line numbers, but a lot of this was from higher bond yields on higher interest rates, which could be coming down in the next year or two. But their portfolio size has increased, and if rates remain elevated, so too will this part of their income.

Conclusion

Allstate has done a fine job in profitable progress and has seen big-time recent earnings hikes. They have seen normal growth but, in my opinion, are lacking in growth where it matters most in this industry. I expect that they will post another strong earnings report at the end of the month. For me, before I can consider the stock a buy, I want to see expansion in net premiums earned and the auto side of the business. This is the biggest market in the industry and where other peers separate themselves from ALL. This stock is sort of humdrum for me and worries me with its cyclicality based on weather, inflation, and interest rates. Hold ALL and consider a buy if net premiums advance or a sell if valuation steepens on missed earnings.

data.title: Premium Brands: I Waited A Year For This Dip
data.description:
Finally, after almost a year babysitting a ‘Hold’ on Premium Brands (PBH:CA)(PRBZF), the stock dropped right into that ~$80–$90 entry range I talked about before.

That’s a pretty sharp drop, from ~$105 at the end of February to ~$85 today, but it feels mostly noise to me.

And no, I don’t think a ~$14 million revenue miss this quarter was enough to justify it.

Just to remind you, this stock caught my attention for three reasons:

  1. Premium Brands has gradually become a serial acquirer and is positioning itself in high-growth products (CPG, sandwiches, and bakery, especially in the U.S.).
  2. They've put approximately $1.21 billion in Capex over the past four years and are still running near 4x leverage, but management said they should gain operational leverage over time. EBITDA margins at approximately 6% today can move toward approximately 10–11% adjusted by FY 2027 (I'm keeping an eye on the Tennessee plant).
  3. With higher margins post-Capex cycle, they would generate approximately $400 million in potential FCF (EBITDA of $750 million and using a 5-year average EBITDA-to-OCF conversion of approximately 53%). That's when it really turns into a cash cow.

Okay, Pedro. You probably already know this if you've read the previous articles. This is the fifth in my coverage, and I'm having a lot of fun, by the way.

This time it's mostly a few tweaks to the portfolio, so let's start there.

M&A: The Reason for the Dip

Remember that huge pipeline I showed you in the previous article?

Well, it's still big.

There are 43 targets with ~$9.4 billion in sales potential. Sure, it's a little smaller than last quarter (54 with $11.4 billion in sales potential).

If you look at the executable (Active + Early categories), there are 7 deals. Almost half of last quarter's (5 deals went to the negotiating table and didn't go through).

And I think this is where they got a little stuck.

I think the market wanted to see more advanced M&As in the pipeline. Since they are the 'consolidators,' the $10 billion story may have become a little clouded.

Here's a peek at how the pipeline rolled this quarter:

It's clear that the achievable potential has decreased significantly.

Those 19 executable deals would have given them something close to $3.7 billion in incremental revenue (to scoop that up at ~0.7 to 1x sales, they would need to spend something above $2.5 billion).

Now they’re sitting on about $761 million in incremental revenue from executable deals (~$646 million if they tried to take it all down at once). You might say that’s not a big deal, M&A comes and goes.

But we know Mr. Market, always a bit jumpy.

One Enters, Another Leaves

Even with the pipeline slowing a bit, there were still two portfolio moves this quarter, and since I only touched on Stampede last time, it's worth digging into it a bit more here.

Right off the bat, it’s pretty clear this acquisition plugs some gaps in their growth strategy.

  • Premium Brands adds over 600,000 sq ft of capacity in the U.S.
  • They can use procurement capacity in their new markets and in their high-growth categories (Protein and Sandwich).
  • It expands their customer base, since Stampede is foodservice-heavy, unlike Premium Brands, whose core business is more retail.

Looking at the numbers, Stampede pulled in US$936 million in revenue in FY 2025, with a CAGR of ~11.5% over three years. Higher than Premium Brands' 7.4% over the same stretch.

Adjusted EBITDA is US$68 million. A margin of ~7.2%.

Well, considering that Premium Brands reported it at ~9% in FY 2025, it's a bit dilutive, but I imagine the synergies will make it accretive next year.

But of course, everything has a price.

Stampede's was ~US$688 million ($952.4 million already converted to Canadian dollars). So, we're talking about a transaction of ~10x EBITDA, but something closer to 7.5x after synergies.

This is management implicitly telling us that there's at least $23.7 million in synergies on the table.

Not bad, if you ask me.

But how they made that acquisition is a bit complicated.

It was approximately US$512.5 million raised from equity (the largest chunk in public subscription receipts and some private placement receipts), convertible debentures at 5.5%, and a small amount of revolving debt.

Sounds complicated, I know. But what's most important here is that they ended up issuing approximately 7.2 million shares.

You can see that already in the balance sheet.

That's actually good.

They're using expensive equity to protect their already leveraged balance sheet in order to expand.

And honestly, I think the biggest factor here is Stampede's approximately 40% unused capacity in the U.S.

By my back-of-the-envelope calculations, if they use 100% capacity, sales could expand to something close to US$1.5 billion. An incremental EBITDA of approximately US$108 million (almost $150 million converted), 32% of their current annual EBITDA.

Of course, I don't expect 100% capacity utilization, but any incrementality in that is already something that isn't priced in.

Before I forget, the second deal I mentioned earlier was Premium Brands selling 74% of its stake in Shaw Bakers.

As Shaw Bakers alone had sales of $170 million, and they sold it for $114 million (remember, 74%), they valued it at almost $155 million. A sales multiple of ~0.9x sales. They didn't sell it cheaply compared to other deals.

It was bigger than the Stampede deal, for example.

But do you know what the most interesting part is?

In the Stampede deal, about $102 million was financed by the revolver. With this sale, they can already pay it off without touching the FCF.

Tweaking Some Estimates

Up until now, Premium Brands had grown through debt, but this acquisition of Stampede gave me some indication that they will start using more equity.

Well, that opened a gap in my previous model.

I was even projecting some buybacks and debt-funded acquisitions along the way. I better take a second look here and see if that target of ~$110 really holds up.

I'll change my assumptions a little too.

To begin with, Wall St. was expecting revenue of $11.2 billion for FY 2028. Now, they are targeting something close to $10.72 billion.

Since management has already guided something between $9.25 and $9.55 billion in FY 2026, growth to the $10.13 billion of FY 2027 would be 7.7% and then ~10%.

It doesn't seem that difficult to me.

Consider that by FY 2025 they've already hit over a 15% mark and still have Stampede's idle capacity to fill.

I'll also use the mid-term adjusted EBITDA margin of 10% that they also guided (adjusted EBITDA at $1.01 billion for FY 2028).

Now, for the shares outstanding, I need to do some calculations.

Just to be clear before we begin. They have ~52 million shares outstanding post-Stampede deal. So, since they should be targeting revenue of ~$9.4 billion at the midpoint for FY 2026, there's a gap of at least $1.32 billion to reach $10.72 billion.

If I consider that they typically scoop up companies at approximately 0.7x revenue, Premium Brands would need something close to $924 million for M&A going forward.

Using the Stampede deal as a base and assuming they take 75% of that in equity, we're talking about $693 million to issue.

At approximately $90, that's 7.7 million more shares to include in my model going forward. I'll consider a range of 6.9 to 8.6 million to bake fluctuations in the stock price.

That's something between 58.9 and 60.6 million shares outstanding by the end of FY 2028.

Also, to be conservative, I'll use net debt at 3x EBITDA (approximately $3.03 billion).

For the multiples, I'll use 10x EBITDA here again.

And finally, I'll bring it to present value at a rate of 7.5% (risk-free rate of 3.4%, beta of 0.88 and an expected return of ~8%).

This all gives me a price target of between $100 and $104 for Premium Brands. Basically ~$5 lower than my last price target.

And folks, I might be underestimating them a bit this time, since I'm using full debt capacity ($3.03 billion) while also using 'maximum' dilution for M&A.

And that might not be the case.

Either they use debt or equity, and judging by the way things are going, they'll likely prefer to use equity.

If the debt remains somewhat more controlled (~2.5x EBITDA or $2.52 billion), the price target could stretch to something close to $108 to $112, depending on the dilution.

Anyway, at $85 the stock gives me at least 17% up to $100, which is the low point of my most conservative scenario. And if we add in the dividend yield of ~4%, that's more than 20% total return on the table in two years.

But if we aim for $110 as before (the scenario I just showed you), the total return is almost 35% with dividends.

I wouldn't rule that out. If they keep growing organically in a strong U.S. market, they won't need to lean as much on dilution or debt.

I'm going to change my rating here to 'Buy'.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Fidelity National Financial: Housing Pessimism Is Too Extreme (Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Fidelity National Financial (FNF) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about a quarter of their value. In particular, shares have weakened over the past six weeks as interest rates have risen, which has dampened hopes for a boom in refinancing transactions. At the same time, FNF’s stake in F&G Annuities & Life (FG) has lost value amid concerns around private credit. I last covered Fidelity in November, rating shares a “Hold,” but with the stock down 18% since then, a “Sell” was merited. After such a decline and with a softer macro environment, now is a good time to revisit FNF to see if investors should buy the dip or if more weakness lies ahead.

First off, when valuing Fidelity, I value its stand-alone business and add back the market value of its F&G stake. Now, in December, FNF distributed 16.3 million (or ~12% of the company) shares of F&G to its shareholders. After this sale, it still owns about 70% of the company, which is worth about $2.25 billion. This stake is worth about $8.25 per FNF share. I would also note that I have covered F&G separately for investors interested in its stand-alone prospects.

Now, when entering 2026, there was substantial hope that we would see growing title volumes. FNF generates premiums during both refinancing and purchase activity; as a general rule of thumb, when there is a new mortgage, it is an opportunity to sell title insurance. As interest rates approached 6%, this created the opportunity for both a refi wave and an increase in purchase transactions (which tend to be more lucrative).

As you can see below, however, rates have ticked back up, and the increase in oil prices is likely to delay Fed rate cuts and keep longer-term yields higher. Beyond the rate impact, the renewed bout of uncertainty may cause some potential homebuyers to hold off on that purchase decision, especially with the labor market softening somewhat.

Back in February, FNF was hoping for existing home sales to be up around 10%, assuming rates remain close to 6%. I expect a level of purchase transactions that is up only modestly this year, 0%-3%, as this rate surge occurred at the worst possible time, just before the start of the spring selling season. That said, we should see healthy refinance activity in Q1, thanks to the drop in rates, which should buoy full-year results. I expect the pace of refinancing to slow sequentially. We appear set for another year with below-average mortgage and title activity.

Looking at recent results, in its fourth quarter reported on February 19, Fidelity missed expectations by $0.09, earning $1.41 per share. Note that these results include F&G, which posted mixed Q4 earnings. Its title business earned $401 million in pre-tax profits ($306 million after tax) with margins running at 17.5%. For the full year 2025, margins expanded 80bps to 15.9%. This business has fairly significant operating leverage, as it can better utilize its sales force when mortgage activity is increasing. As you can see below, revenues have risen about 10% in each of the past two years.

That said, volumes are still well below 2021 peak levels, and adjusted for home price inflation, they are running below pre-COVID norms too. While FNF has made progress in enhancing margins, title margins are still below 2019 levels, even as revenue is slightly higher. This reflects an overhang from staff added during the 2020-2022 boom that the company has been working through. The margin improvement seen in Q4 is an optimistic sign that FNF can generate better margins sequentially in 2026, even on a similar level of volumes, especially as the company continues to invest in technology and AI to automate work.

While I expect the residential market to remain sluggish this year, I remain optimistic about commercial activity. There was a meaningful rebound in 2025, and we are nearly back to record levels. Capital markets access has improved, enabling more transactions and refinancings to occur. With more employers moving away from remote working, a worst-case outcome for offices is less likely, and this should enable healthy volumes to continue in 2026. Additionally, the growth in data center construction is likely to provide a tailwind for commercial title insurance revenue.

With only modest growth in residential activity, FNF will be dependent on a continuation of commercial activity to support growth. I expect this to occur, though at less than 20% of the business, residential trends matter much more. Given my view that mortgage rates are unlikely to sustainably break below 6%, I expect title revenue to be up 0%-5% in 2026.

As is common in the title insurance industry, FNF’s own $5 billion investment portfolio (excluding F&G) is extremely low risk. Just 1% sits in structured products, and FNF’s portfolio does not have the private credit exposure that has been a source of such fear in the marketplace. Nearly half of the portfolio matures in less than a year, so its results are closely tied to the fed funds rate. One area of upside is that FNF expected $95-$100 million of quarterly investment income, assuming two rate cuts. I now expect no more than 1 cut and for investment income to run in the $100-$105 million range.

Management spoke of doing more M&A this year, and there are many small providers of title insurance that can still be rolled up. While FNF is the nation’s leading title insurer, the industry is highly fragmented, providing scope for dealmaking. With 32% market share, though, deals are likely to be bolt-on in nature rather than transformative. To the extent that FNF is active in M&A, it may do fewer buybacks than it otherwise could. Still, I would be surprised if M&A activity exceeded $250 million.

Fidelity has a reasonable balance with 29% debt to capital, a level it has been at for three years. This is the high end of its 205%-30% target. As such, any capital returns are likely to be out of free cash flow. Importantly, the holding company has $695 million of cash, providing ample resources for near-term payouts. Shares offer a 4.4% dividend yield, which I view as secure. During 2025, FNF did $251 million of buybacks, and I expect at least $350 million this year. If FNF does more M&A than I anticipate, buybacks would be below this level.

Back in November, I saw Fidelity earning about $3.75 in 2026 (ex F&G). I still expect full-year earnings to be at least at this level, as I never priced in that large of a mortgage wave, and commercial activity should continue to be a benefit. Plus, higher earnings on its investment portfolio help to partially offset its rate headwind in title policies. Given the slow-growth nature of this business, I view ~12x earnings as appropriate.

That points to a fair value of $45 on a stand-alone basis. Adding in its FG stake, FNF is worth about $53. With about 13% upside and a secure dividend, I believe the recent pullback in shares has finally made FNF cheap enough to buy. As such, I am upgrading shares to a “Buy” and would begin accumulating shares of this quality company at an attractive valuation.

data.title: Perdoceo Education Offers Upside Potential Amid Growth Prospects
data.description:
Lately, I have found myself diving into the world of the for-profit education space. Even though it is not an industry that I, personally, approve of, I do think it is an interesting space. Many of the companies in the industry seem to be growing rapidly, though there is some lumpiness. One interesting prospect worthy of our attention is Perdoceo Education Corporation (PRDO), an enterprise in the industry with a market capitalization of $2.36 billion. That makes it a pretty sizable player in the space. In recent years, even though financial results have been a bit lumpy, the overall trajectory for the business has been positive. Enrollment is growing, and shares are trading at levels that look pretty attractive. I wouldn't say that it's the most appealing player in this market. But it definitely deserves a solid "Buy" rating as far as I am concerned.

Opening the Book on Perdoceo Education Corporation

The management team at Perdoceo Education Corporation describes the company as a provider of post-secondary education. The services that it offers include online programs but can also involve campus-based and hybrid learning programs. At the end of the day, however, the company is really best described by looking at these specific academic institutions that it has in operation. The first of these worth mentioning is Colorado Technical University, or CTU for short. Management describes this as a provider of higher education services to non-traditional adult learners, members of the military, and even traditional students. The specific programs that the firm offers here include, but are not limited to, healthcare management, nursing, computer science, engineering, IT, cybersecurity, criminal justice, business and management, and more.

This is not the only institution that the business has. Though only around half the size in terms of revenue, American InterContinental University System, or AIUS for short, is also incredibly relevant. Through this institution, the company focuses on career-oriented disciplines involving business studies, IT, education, behavioral sciences, and criminal justice. This enterprise consists of three different universities, specifically American InterContinental University, Trident University International, and California Southern University. And then finally, we have the newest of the institutions, known as St. Augustine for Health Services, or USAHS for short. This came into the fold in 2024 and focuses on providing graduate education opportunities in the Health Sciences space, with examples being physical therapy, occupational therapy, speech-language therapy, nursing, and more. It even provides certain continuing education programs for the medical space.

Over the last few years now, Perdoceo Education Corporation has been successful in growing its top line. Even though revenue dropped from $710 million in 2023 to $681.3 million in 2024, it subsequently surged to $847 million in 2025. Management made it very clear that this surge in revenue was because of the acquisition of USAHS. However, it would be a mistake to say that this was the only source of revenue expansion. As the chart below illustrates, enrollment for the company has been consistently climbing year after year. That includes its CTU and AIUS segments.

In the first chart below, you can see overall financial performance for the firm's AIUS segment. Even though enrollment for AIUS has risen over the last three years, both revenue and profitability for it have dropped. Most of this drop occurred from 2023 to 2024, when sales plummeted 11.1%. Management chalked that up to a lag impact during the first three quarters of that year caused by operational changes that were made in the second half of 2023. If this were the only issue, you would expect revenue to have rebounded in 2025. But according to management, the drop in sales experienced there, even in light of the rise in enrollment, was caused by the impact of the government shutdown negatively affecting Trident University. The good news is that we should see a positive outcome this year based on what management has said. And that's because the growth in enrollment year over year was the result of an extra session that started in December of 2025 that should boost sales in 2026.

By comparison, the other operating segments have performed much better. We have seen CTU revenue grow from $456.2 million to $461.6 million, with that largely the result of growing enrollment, not to mention a rise in corporate student programming and higher student retention. This naturally translated to higher profits as well. But the surge in profitability was rather robust, with operating income growing from $150.7 million to $180.6 million. Most of this happened between 2023 and 2024 thanks to management reining in operating expenses as it right-sized its cost structure to provide more simplified professional development offerings. And then finally, the addition of USAHS certainly helped, with revenue growing from nothing back in 2023, since it was not owned at that time, to $157.6 million last year.

Higher revenue naturally translates to higher profits. Over the last three years, net income grew from $147.7 million to $159.9 million. But that's not all. We saw more than a doubling in operating cash flow from $112 million to $225.2 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $201.8 million to $246.6 million. Finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $181.6 million to $237.2 million.

When it comes to the 2026 fiscal year, management has not provided as much data as I would like. But they did provide enough for us to get a good idea of what is going on here. They said, for instance, that adjusted operating income should grow to $263 million. That compares to the $237.6 million reported for 2025. Higher enrollment, driven by the aforementioned extra session, not to mention the rollout of new program versions in certain spaces such as the physical and occupational therapy category, should push overall results higher. Earnings per share should come in at between $2.78 and $2.93. At the midpoint, this would translate to net profits of $188.9 million compared to the $159.9 million reported for last year. If we assume that other profitability metrics rise similarly, we would get adjusted operating cash flow of $291.3 million and EBITDA of $280.7 million.

Using these estimates, we can see in the chart above how shares are priced. Even if we use the 2025 figures instead of the forward estimates for 2026, the stock looks attractive. Particularly noteworthy is the EV to EBITDA multiple that was driven lower by the fact that the business has cash that exceeds debt in the amount of $631.9 million. For a business with a market capitalization of $2.36 billion, that is significant. The chart also shows that shares are cheaper than what we see from other similar enterprises. And to illustrate for you just how meaningful that is from an investment perspective, we need only look at the chart below. In it, you can see how much upside the stock would experience if it were to trade at the multiple of the cheapest of these five enterprises that I compared it to. Admittedly, the upside here is not all that great, at between 2.3% and 4.7%. But if it were to trade at the average of these businesses, which I think is at least warranted considering its balance sheet situation, then upside would range between 27.5% and 98.5%. That is definitely meaningful.

Takeaway

I believe that Perdoceo Education Corporation makes for a solid investment prospect. It's not my favorite player in the industry, but it is deserving of a bullish outlook in light of its strong balance sheet, growth prospects for this year, and financial performance last year. At the end of the day, this all justifies a "Buy" rating in my book.
